## [00:00 - 03:00]

All right, I have some good news for you. Other benefits for a homework for life, ones I never saw coming until I began doing it. You saw my homework for life list from 2015. You saw the highlighted one? Let me tell you that story. It's two o'clock in the morning and I'm asleep when I hear a scratch on my bed. I look and I see my dog Kaylee, a little white lasso opso. You know if you're a dog owner that your dog can sort of look at you and the look can communicate a sentence? Her look to me says, I need to pee. I give her a look back that communicates a sentence. It says, really? It's two o'clock in the morning. You are not a two o'clock in the morning dog who needs to pee. She gives me another look back, which is don't be a jerk. I need to pee. Now I have a decision to make. It's November in New England, but if you live in New England, you know that in November it can be 70 degrees or 10 degrees. This is one of those warm New England nights. All I'm wearing is a pair of boxer shorts. It's actually a satin pair of boxer shorts covered in hearts given to me by my mother-in-law on Valentine's Day, which is something I will never quite understand. They're great to sleep in. I need to decide, am I going to get dressed before I bring my dog outside or am I just going to sort of bring her out onto the lawn? I live on a street that you don't drive on unless you live on it. It's sort of called the Sacky kind of street. There's only about 12 houses in the neighborhood. I know all my neighbors. No one is going to be out on the lawn at two o'clock in the morning. Kaylee pees quickly. She doesn't mess around. I decide, we'll just go out on the lawn real quick. I'll let you pee and then I'll bring you inside. So we go downstairs. I attach her to the leash. I'm out on the lawn. I don't even have shoes on and she pees. And then I go to turn to head back in and she pulls. She wants to go for a walk, which is crazy because she really doesn't like to walk as much anymore. She's getting old, but she wants to walk. She wants to go to the end of the street, which is just four houses away. It's like barely a walk at all to the stop sign. But all I'm wearing is underwear, not even shoes. So I have a decision to make again. Do I bring my dog for this little walk or do I just bring her inside? Unfortunately, I'm the kind of person who can't say no to his pets. I believe that like spoiling pets is a great idea and spoiling children is a terrible idea. And so I can't say no to Kaylee. I say, fine, we'll go for this quick walk. It's dark. There are no stars in the sky on this night. It's sort of like really a weird night. The birds are loud. It's pitch black. I say, fine, we'll walk to the end of the street, which we do. We go down four houses. We hit the stop sign. I turn to pull back and she pulls again.

## [03:00 - 06:02]

She wants to go around the block and I have another decision to make. Now the next street, if we take that right, that's Francis Avenue. There's probably no one on that street either. I'll be okay even though I'm in underwear. But when I make the next right, I'll be on Main Street in Newington, Connecticut. There will be cars and trucks and maybe a police officer driving up and down that street. People will see me in my underwear. So I look down at myself and I see guys sometimes sort of running around with just a pair of shorts and no shirt on. I don't look like those guys. I know that, but maybe someone drives by they could imagine he's out running with his dog and maybe he's running at two o'clock in the morning because that's the way he looks and he doesn't want people to see him. For whatever reason, I decide this will be okay. I will quickly walk around the block with my dog in my underwear and everything's going to be fine. And so we walk down Francis Avenue. We make the right onto Main Street in Newington. I'm as far away from my house as I possibly can be when it's that moment when it wasn't raining a second ago. And now all the rain that could possibly fall in one moment is pouring down on us. And I suddenly understand why there were no stars in the sky because there were clouds. I understand why the birds were so loud because they knew it was coming. I even kind of understand why Kaylee wanted to go around the block. We used to run in the rain all the time. She loved being out in the rain, but she's an old dog now and we haven't been out in the rain in a really long time. She probably knew the rain was coming too. She probably dragged my ass to this corner so she could experience the rain with me. And if I wasn't doing homework for life, what would happen next would probably be this. I'd pick Kaylee up in a huff. I'd storm home. I'd put her down on the floor, take a shower, and a year later, that memory would be lost to me forever, as most memories are lost to most people. But because I'm doing homework for life, because I have a lens for storytelling, thank God I have a lens for storytelling that night. I look down at Kaylee and I recognize that she's 18 years old. She's so old that truthfully every morning I awaken, I look to make sure that she's still breathing. She's that old. And as I look down at her, giving me that big doggy smile, I realize this might be the last time I ever walk in the rain with my best friend. And because of that, because I see this moment for what it is, because I've been looking at my life through story for so long, I stand there on Main Street in my underwear in the

## [06:02 - 09:09]

rain with my dog until she is ready to go, until this moment is over for her. And then this other amazing thing is going to happen to you, maybe the most amazing of all the things about homework for life. You'll also crack open. Suddenly memories from the past, ones you've left behind, ones you can't believe you have left behind, will return to you. As you begin seeing the narrative of the day, the narratives of the past will return. And so standing on that corner for a moment, I'm no longer looking at Kaylee. And instead the memory of Measelman returns. Measelman, my very first dog, the dog named after the doctor who gave my father his vasectomy, Dr. Measelman. When my parents got divorced when I was seven, my father essentially got kicked out of the house by my mother and he was immediately replaced by another man. My father was a cowboy in every sense of the word. I grew up on a horse farm. But when my parents got divorced, my father lost the house he bought from his parents. He lost the barn he built with his own hands. He lost the horses because he no longer had the land to raise them. He lost his children because I really haven't had a relationship with my father since that divorce. He lost his wife to another man. But standing on that corner that night in my underwear in the rain, it occurred to me for the first time that my father also lost his dog in the divorce because Measelman, his dog, stayed with us. The man who took his wife and his kids and his home also took his dog. And somehow that night on that corner, it felt like the worst loss of all. It was the first time I had ever seen the losses of my father through the eyes of a man rather than the eyes of a boy. Something that I had never realized until that moment. And then that memory is wiped away almost instantly by another one that swells up. And in this memory, I'm on the 18th green at the Walpole Country Club as a teenager, an 18 year old. I'm naked with a girl on the green when the sprinklers come on at midnight dumping more water on us than I could ever imagine. And because I'm on a public corner in Newington, Connecticut, practically naked in the rain, that memory of being naked in public under those sprinklers has suddenly returned to me. A memory I had completely forgotten until that night. All of those things happened to me on that corner because I'm doing homework for life. It changes the way I see my life and my days and it returns the past to me and profound

## [09:09 - 11:28]

and exciting and deeply fulfilling ways. And thank goodness that all of those things happened and that I saw the moment for what it was because it ended up being the last time I walked in the rain with Kaylee. She died three months later. And that walk in my underwear in the rain with my friend will stay with me forever. And the beauty of storytelling is that as I told that story, suddenly the camera I'm staring at disappeared and the room that I'm in went away. And for a little bit of time, I was back on that corner and I could see my friend who I miss every day. I could see my friend so clearly in my mind's eye. I told a story to myself and suddenly I was back in that moment enjoying the company of a friend who I loved for so long. We are the most important audiences for our stories because we can do the things that I just did for myself. We can bring back the past. We can put it in the present for a moment and enjoy it. We can remember the things that are worthy of remembering. Homework for life will change the way you see your life. Homework for life will change the way you live your life. It'll bring back the past in the most unexpected ways at the most unexpected moments and it will fill your heart with joy that you have connected the life you're living now with the life you once lived and the two of them will become one. Homework for life will not only allow you to gather the stories you're living today, but it will bring back the stories of the past, the stories that you were living before homework for life. And because of that, you will feel richer and fuller and more complete than you have ever felt before. I promise you. Homework for life has so many benefits, so many ways to make you a happier and a better person.
